text.title: Agent Pitfalls and Rename Guidance
text.summary: Common agent-generated token organization mistakes, aliasing depth limits, and rename codemod guidance.

antipattern.description: LLM generates full Cartesian product of state × variant tokens (`color.brand.primary.light.hover.disabled`).
antipattern.reason: Symmetric token trees balloon to hundreds of tokens; cap depth and require justification per token in the prompt. Most generated tokens are never referenced.

antipattern.description: Agent suggests component tokens that duplicate existing semantic tokens.
antipattern.reason: Duplicate component tokens double the rename surface area and confuse consumers about which to use; require an explicit justification for each component token.

antipattern.description: Agent prunes "unused" tokens automatically via static analysis.
antipattern.reason: Static analysis misses theming, runtime, per-tenant, and Storybook MDX references; auto-pruning causes silent regressions.

antipattern.description: Agent invents a naming convention name ("Carbon style", "MUI v6 style") without grounding in the project's actual convention.
antipattern.reason: Pin the convention regex in every agent prompt; confidently invented convention names produce non-compliant output.

rule.statement: Enforce a maximum aliasing depth of 3 levels; validate in CI.
rule.rationale: Aliasing depth beyond 3 makes runtime debugging require tracing through multiple files; visual diffs become the only verification method.

rule.statement: When renaming tokens, include MDX, Storybook configs, and JSON data files in the scan — not just TypeScript/JS/CSS.
rule.rationale: Agents default to code-file globs and miss Storybook MDX stories, documentation JSON, and Figma plugin config files where token names appear as strings.

rule.statement: Pair every rename with a Storybook visual diff to catch unreferenced usages that the text scan missed.
rule.rationale: A renamed token with a surviving reference at the visual level indicates a scan gap; the visual diff catches what grep-based tools miss.
